summary: Fernando Pérez traces the evolution of IPython and Jupyter from a personal terminal tool in 2001 to a global platform for interactive computing used by scientists, educators, and industry. He emphasizes the ethical imperative of building open tools, particularly for researchers in developing countries who cannot afford proprietary software licenses. The talk highlights Jupyter's architecture inspired by web protocols, its expansion to support over 100 programming languages, and its role in landmark scientific achievements like the LIGO gravitational wave detection. Pérez also discusses the transformative impact of Jupyter on education at UC Berkeley, where Data 8 and Data 100 courses are reaching over half the campus. He closes with reflections on representation, mentorship, and the value of non-academic career paths.
key_points: Building open scientific tools has an ethical dimension: researchers in developing countries are excluded when science depends on expensive proprietary software
Jupyter's architecture mirrors the web — a standardized protocol decoupled from any single language, enabling over 100 language kernels and broad community adoption
The LIGO gravitational wave discovery was published entirely with open source tools and reproducible Jupyter Notebooks, vindicating a community that in 2008 was struggling to justify its existence
UC Berkeley's Data 8 and Data 100 courses are reaching roughly half the campus, making computational fluency as baseline as email for undergraduates across all majors
Sustainable open source requires deliberate institution-building — NumFOCUS, BIDS, and cross-sector funding from foundations and industry, not just traditional academic grants
money_quotes: If we consider that part of the mission of scientific research is to open the black box of nature and understand how it works, it's antithetical to that mission to perform that job with tools that themselves we are legally prevented from inspecting.

Everything that I will be talking about today, even though it is in the context of complex scientific questions, our realm is that tries to build tools for the limited brain bucket that we all have that isn't getting any bigger or any faster.
In 2008, we were justifying that we had a right to be there in the first place.
Sometimes you have to make your own spaces. You have to create them because they're not exactly going to give them to you.
I was literally told, stop doing this. You're wasting your career. Get back to writing physics and math papers.
Even if students, for financial reasons, don't have a good laptop, it doesn't matter, because they walk to the library, they borrow a Chromebook, and they can do everything off a Chromebook.
At the end of the day, it's all about the brains and the ideas, and on that dimension, we are equal.
If you're in a bad relationship, walk away and walk away early.
key_insight: Open tools for science are not merely a technical preference but an ethical imperative — they democratize access, enable reproducibility, and build human communities that proprietary software structurally cannot.
